items.q: What does natural law theory base morality on?
items.correct: Human nature and reason — there are objective purposes (telos) built into humans which reason can discover, generating universal moral precepts.
items.misconception: Natural law means whatever happens in nature is morally right.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is the same thing as the laws of physics.
items.why: The 'naturalistic fallacy' confusion — students assume natural law endorses lions eating gazelles or natural disasters. Aquinas's theory concerns human nature and rational ends, not all natural events.

items.q: What does the cosmological argument try to prove?
items.correct: That the existence of the universe requires a first cause or necessary being — usually identified with God — to explain why anything exists at all.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument proves who created life on Earth.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument proves the Big Bang happened.
items.why: Students mix up cosmology (science) with the cosmological argument (philosophy). The argument is about why anything exists at all, not the specific origins of life or the Big Bang.

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: Is 'do good and avoid evil' one of the five primary precepts?
items.correct: No — it is the synderesis rule, the self-evident first principle of practical reason; the five primary precepts (life, reproduction, education, society, worship) are derived from it.
items.misconception: 'Do good and avoid evil' is the first of the five primary precepts.
items.random_wrong: 'Do good and avoid evil' is a secondary precept that varies between cultures.
items.why: Getting the architecture right matters: synderesis grounds the precepts, the precepts ground secondary rules. Listing six 'primary precepts' signals a structural misunderstanding to examiners.

items.q: Does following natural law mean copying what happens in the natural world?
items.correct: No — natural law is the rational creature's participation in eternal law: morality is discovered by reason reflecting on human nature's proper ends, not by imitating animal behaviour.
items.misconception: Natural law means humans should behave as animals do in nature, since that is natural.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is another name for the law of gravity applied to ethics.
items.why: Lions kill rivals' cubs; nothing follows for human ethics. Aquinas's 'nature' is teleological and rational. The colloquial reading generates pseudo-objections the theory easily survives.

items.q: Which is NOT one of Aquinas' four kinds of law?
items.why: Correct answer: Cosmic law. “Eternal law” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many primary precepts does Aquinas identify?
items.why: Correct answer: Five. “Four” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In which part of the Summa Theologica are the questions on law (90-97) located?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
